nativeRange.habitat: The Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang inhabits reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs, typically over coral, rock, pavement, and rubble substrates. It ranges from the surface down to around 60 m, though it is most commonly encountered between 6 and 30 m. Among the bristletooth tangs of the genus Ctenochaetus, this species has the widest natural range and is the only member that extends into the Red Sea.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
moderate to strong water flow (10–20x turnover)
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift, sort, and scrape algal films, detritus, and small invertebrates from hard substrate. Marine-based seaweed (nori, ogo) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, brine) alongside tankmates. Cannot survive on live-rock grazing alone — supplemental algae feeding is commonly recommended. Vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5436x3624. Tack-sharp full-body side profile on clean background; clearly shows the fine wavy blue-grey lines running head to tail, orange eye/head spots, and yellow pectoral fin. Best diagnostic shot.
Diego Delso, 4308x2872. Sharp in-situ Red Sea shot of a warm orange-toned adult; vivid orange head spots and fine white striae, whole animal in frame. Shows warm colour variant.
Julien Bidet (MDC Seamarc Maldives), 2086x1568. Sharp full side profile of a classic olive-brown adult with fine lines; represents the darker olive morph described in the summary.

summary: A widely distributed Indo-Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with an olive-brown body, fine wavy blue lines, and orange spots near the eyes. Generally considered reef-safe and a manageable choice for medium-to-large reef systems, though it tends to be aggressive toward other bristletooth tangs and is susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion.
careNotes:
The Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus striatus) is the most widely distributed member of the bristletooth genus, ranging from the Red Sea and East Africa across the Indo-Pacific to French Polynesia, though it does not extend to Hawaii, the Marquesas, or Easter Island.

A tank of around 500 L (approximately 130 gallons) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. While some sources set the minimum closer to 125–150 gallons, 500 L is the more cautious recommendation given this species' status as one of the larger Ctenochaetus, its need for open swimming space, strong flow (10–20x turnover is commonly recommended), and abundant live rock for natural grazing routes.

Water parameters should be stable and within standard reef ranges (specific gravity approximately 1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH).

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed such as nori or ogo, along with spirulina-based herbivore foods, is generally advised at least three times per week. Vitamin-enriched diets are commonly suggested to reduce the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition in which pits and discoloration develop along the head and lateral line — which this species, like most surgeonfish, is reported to develop under poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet.

The species is generally considered reef-safe and is best kept singly per tank. It tends to be highly intolerant of other Ctenochaetus tangs and its own kind, but aggression toward unrelated reef fish is typically modest, especially when all fish are introduced together. Juveniles display shimmering blue-grey bodies with pronounced wide orange body stripes and orange spots around the eyes; adults transition to an olive-brown body with fine wavy blue lines running from head to tail, retaining the orange eye spots.

As an acanthurid (surgeonfish), this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades — the bony spine near the base of the tail known as the 'scalpel' — which are used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. Spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and group-bonding constraints. Sexes are not visually distinct in the aquarium trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus cf. striatus). Provided max size (10 in / ~25 cm), tank minimum (150 gal / 568 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root after specific URL was located via search (the collection landing page does not directly list every species).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus striatus Quoy & Gaimard, 1825), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (26.0 cm TL, common 18.0 cm), depth range (0–60 m, typical 6–30 m), preferred temperature (24.6–29°C, mean 27.8°C), Indo-Pacific native range including the Red Sea (excluding Hawaiian, Marquesas, Easter), habitat (reef flats, lagoons, seaward reefs over coral/rock/pavement/rubble), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and reproduction (lunar spawning in large schools, maturity ~10+ cm).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Retailer secondary used for tank-size triangulation. Confirmed max size ~10 in, minimum tank 125 gal (~473 L; slightly smaller than LiveAquaria's 150 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (moderate), semi-aggressive, reef-safe.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Wholesale source / care article. Confirmed minimum tank 125 gal with strong flow (10–20x turnover), juvenile vs adult coloration (blue-grey juvenile with orange stripes → olive adult with fine blue lines and orange eye spots), aquarium adult size 7–8 in, peaceful with non-conspecifics when introduced together, intolerant of other Bristletooth Tangs, generally peaceful with corals/clams. Notes the species shreds but does not eat hair algae.

additional.name: Reef App
additional.notes: Hobbyist database secondary used for tank-size and temperament triangulation. Listed tank volume 500 L (~132 gal), max size 26 cm, reef-safe, peaceful-to-mildly-aggressive temperament, herbivorous diet on micro- and macroalgae with supplementary frozen meaty foods, average hardiness.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records have been found in available hobby sources. In the wild, the species is a pelagic broadcast spawner that has been documented spawning in large schools during lunar-phase events; aquarium spawning is considered highly unlikely.
